Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Right Fast, averaging 131.4 kph (up to 139.0).
  • Typical length is 6-7m (their good length); median 7.0 m.
  • Stock ball is a good length wide outside off, swinging away, seaming away, passing outside off (18% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 46% of the time against left-handers.
  • Round the wicket he shifts his pitching line from stumps to wide of 6th.
  • Across an over he varies his length a lot, banging it in more as the over goes on.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Good Length (6 wkts, 1.3% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Wide of 6th.
  • Takes 18% of wickets pitching a good length down the leg side.
  • Beats the bat 6.1% of tracked balls (false-shot 12.3%).
  • Gets you caught behind more than most — 82% of his wickets (1.2× the typical rate for this type).
  • 67% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to keeper, gully, mid on: silly.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching a good length on the stumps (12% of runs).
  • Short ball: 33% of deliveries, economy 3.5 — 3 wkts from 273 short balls.
  • Away from good length, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (46%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes full wide outside off (economy 5.7, beats the bat only 5%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs left-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — a good length wide outside off, swinging away, seaming away, passing outside off (18% of deliveries, economy 1.92). Minor variations: a good length on the stumps (16%) and a good length down the leg side (11%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
a good length wide outside off ▶swinging away, seaming away18%1.9217%
a good length on the stumps ▶swinging away, seaming both ways16%2.74223%
a good length down the leg side ▶swinging away, seaming both ways11%2.51215%
a good length outside off ▶swinging away, seaming both ways9%2.05115%
full wide outside off ▶swinging away9%5.6735%
back of a length wide outside off ▶seaming away, swinging both ways6%4.6727%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Most threatening pitching a good length wide outside off (6 wkts, 1.3% strike). Most wickets come from a good length down the leg side, while he leaks the most runs a good length on the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs left-handers)

60% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 12 balls; most runs go to the leg side (46%); the drive hurts most (34 fours/sixes at 2.7 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at 5th stump — pitches around wide of 6th and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the off side (50%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it a good length wide outside off most often (18%); drops short often (33%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched a good length down the leg side, but strikes most often pitching wide outside off.

Speed & Spells

He holds his pace across spells, is a touch slower in the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.
Speed by Spell
By spell — opening burst vs later spells.
Speed by Innings
1st vs 2nd innings of the match.
Speed by Day
By match day — fatigue across the game.

Round the wicket his pitching line moves from stumps to wide of 6th — economy 3.45→3.35, a wicket every 89→63 balls (over→round).

Sequencing & Over Construction

Variable with his length (length spread ±2.6 m; more repeatable than 13% of pace bowlers); tends to bang it in later in the over (short 9%→13% by the 5th–6th ball); and holds the same crease position through the over.
Across 28 dismissals, his wicket ball is close in length and pace to the delivery before it — he builds pressure with repetition, not a big change-up.
Ball in overMedian lengthShort %EconWkt %

Ball 66.6 m10%4.472.8%
Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 82% of right-arm pace); releases tight to the stumps (49 cm out, tighter than 94% of right-arm pace). Comes wider round the wicket (49 cm over vs 97 cm round).

Movement (vs the average pace bowler · vs left-handers)

A hit-the-deck bowler who bangs it into a hard length for steep bounce.
Generates below avg seam and below avg swing for a pace bowler; seams it away more to left-hand batters (61%).

Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
